project_name: Ethiopia- Expressway Development Support Project
sensitivity_rationale: The PAD provides a standard country context focused on economic growth and demographics, with no conflict or fragility diagnostic. Do-no-harm analysis is absent despite acknowledged resettlement and social disruption risks. Stakeholder engagement is framed around multi-donor coordination and safeguards compliance, not political economy or conflict sensitivity.
responsiveness_rationale: The PDO is a conventional transport efficiency objective with no FCV dimension. Design, targeting, and implementation arrangements follow standard infrastructure practice with no conflict-sensitive adaptations. The results framework contains no FCV-adjusted indicators and adaptive management provisions address only fiduciary and capacity risks.
key_finding: This transport infrastructure PAD operates entirely outside an FCV framework, with no conflict diagnostic, no do-no-harm analysis, and no FCV-adjusted objectives or indicators. Ethiopia was not on the WB FCV list in 2015, but the project's complete absence of conflict sensitivity — despite acknowledged resettlement, ethnic-regional governance complexity, and corridor-level social disruption — places it firmly in the Low FCV integration quadrant.

dimensions.name: FCV Context and Diagnostics
dimensions.key_quote: Ethiopia has a federal, democratic government system...The Ethiopian People's Revolutionary Democratic Front (EPRDF) has been in power since 1991. EPRDF comprises four regionally based parties from the four major regions.
dimensions.rationale: The country context describes Ethiopia's federal-ethnic governance structure and population pressures but frames these solely in economic development terms. There is no conflict risk analysis, no reference to localized fragility or inter-ethnic tensions, and no FCV diagnostic informing project design.

dimensions.name: Do No Harm and Conflict Risk
dimensions.key_quote: Service providers in smaller towns along the existing road would consider they will lose business. The highway will restrict movement of crossing traffic and animals.
dimensions.rationale: The PAD acknowledges social disruption to existing road-side communities and resettlement of farmland and scattered households, but frames these as safeguards compliance issues rather than conflict or do-no-harm risks. No conflict sensitivity lens is applied to displacement, livelihood loss, or corridor access restrictions.

dimensions.name: Stakeholder and Political Economy
dimensions.key_quote: ERA has prepared a Framework for Project Implementation Coordination (FPIC) outlining the arrangements for joint monitoring of compatibility of the engineering standard of the works and consistent implementation of the safeguard instruments.
dimensions.rationale: Stakeholder engagement focuses on multi-donor coordination and safeguards implementation among government agencies. There is no political economy analysis, no assessment of elite capture risks in corridor investments, and no engagement strategy for conflict-affected or marginalized populations in the project area.

dimensions.name: Objectives and Theory of Change
dimensions.key_quote: The objective of the Project is to enhance efficiency and safety in the movement of goods and people along the Batu (Zeway)-Arsi Negele section of the Modjo-Hawassa development corridor.
dimensions.rationale: The PDO is a standard transport efficiency and institutional capacity objective with no FCV-related components. The theory of change links road construction to trade facilitation and economic growth, with no pathway addressing fragility drivers, social cohesion, or conflict-sensitive development.

dimensions.name: Design and Targeting
dimensions.key_quote: Direct beneficiaries within the project influence area between Batu (Zeway) and Arsi Negele are estimated to be about 400,000 of which approximately 50 percent are female.
dimensions.rationale: Beneficiary targeting is based on geography and gender disaggregation with no FCV-adapted approach. The greenfield highway alignment and livelihood restoration sub-component address safeguards obligations but are not designed to prioritise conflict-affected or marginalised populations. No FCV-sensitivity is embedded in design choices.

dimensions.name: Implementation and Operational Flexibility
dimensions.key_quote: As multiple donors will finance separate sections of the road, and this is a new greenfield development...the risk at preparation and implementation is considered to be substantial.
dimensions.rationale: Implementation risk management addresses fiduciary, capacity, and environmental/social safeguards. There are no conflict-contingency provisions, no adaptive protocols for FCV-related disruptions, and no flexibility mechanisms tied to changes in the security or political environment.

dimensions.name: Results Framework and Adaptive Management
dimensions.key_quote: Expected outcomes include: (a) a reduction in travel time between Batu (Zeway) and Arsi Negele; (b) a reduction in vehicle operating cost...and (f) the production of annual reports on citizens' engagement.
dimensions.rationale: The results framework comprises entirely standard transport sector indicators — travel time, vehicle operating cost, road accident fatalities, and beneficiary counts. There are no FCV-adjusted indicators, no resilience or social cohesion metrics, and no adaptive management provisions linked to conflict or fragility dynamics.

dimensions.name: One WBG Integration
dimensions.key_quote: Joint IFC: No
dimensions.rationale: IFC/MIGA involvement is explicitly absent. While the PAD notes China EXIM Bank parallel co-financing and references IFC/MIGA alignment with the Country Partnership Strategy's Pillar One, there is no joint WBG instrument, no IFC private sector engagement, and no MIGA guarantee component.

project_name: Enhancing Shared Prosperity through Equitable Services
sensitivity_rationale: All three Sensitivity dimensions are rated Partially Addressed. The document acknowledges the CDP/Gambella harm pathway (a specific, documented FCV-proximate risk) and describes mitigation measures, but this is reactive rather than proactive. There is no broader FCV diagnostic of Ethiopia's subnational fragility, no RRA or SCD grounding, and no analysis of how the IGFT system may reproduce ethnic or regional exclusion. Donor coordination is described but political economy analysis is absent. No red flags are triggered. The Partially Addressed rating reflects the absence of a genuine FCV analytical framework while crediting the CDP response as specific, documented sensitivity.
responsiveness_rationale: Responsiveness scores are mixed: Design and Targeting reaches Substantially Addressed through meaningful geographic equity targeting and IGFT allocation to underdeveloped regions. However, the theory of change is technocratic with no FCV logic, implementation arrangements lack conflict-specific adaptations (no CERC, no third-party monitoring, no security provisions), the results framework has no FCV indicators or adaptive management mechanisms, and IFC/MIGA integration is absent. The P4R instrument is appropriate for an institutional capacity-building agenda but is not framed or adapted for a moderately fragile context.
key_finding: ESPES is a well-designed equity-focused service delivery operation that addresses geographic and wealth disparities through the IGFT system, but it engages with FCV dynamics only reactively through the Inspection Panel CDP response. The absence of an FCV diagnostic, FCV-informed theory of change, conflict-adapted implementation arrangements, and FCV indicators in the results framework places this operation in the Low FCV integration cell, which is a meaningful gap for a $600 million operation in a Tier 2 FCV context.

dimensions.name: FCV Context and Diagnostics
dimensions.rationale: The document presents Ethiopia's governance and development context in considerable depth but frames it almost entirely through a development performance and decentralisation lens, with no FCV diagnostic. The only substantive FCV-proximate content is the Inspection Panel case around the CDP/Gambella forced relocation, which is handled defensively rather than as a systemic FCV context analysis. There is no reference to Ethiopia's subnational conflict dynamics, ethnic tensions, displacement trends, or relevant diagnostics such as the RRA or SCD. Geographic disparities are identified but interpreted as poverty differentials, not as FCV drivers.

dimensions.name: Do No Harm and Conflict Risk Identification
dimensions.rationale: The document acknowledges the CDP/Gambella harm pathway explicitly and describes a screening tool and Management Action Plan as mitigation. This represents a specific, documented harm pathway response, which is meaningful. However, the harm pathway analysis is entirely backward-looking (responding to the PBS 3 Inspection Panel findings) rather than a proactive assessment of risks the ESPES operation itself could generate. No systematic analysis of how block grant disbursements through government systems could reinforce exclusion, enable elite capture, or fund harmful programmes in conflict-affected subnational areas. The mitigation measures are primarily procedural (a screening tool) rather than design-level adaptations.

dimensions.name: Stakeholder and Political Economy Analysis
dimensions.rationale: Donor coordination is well-described and substantive. Government commitment is assessed through financing data showing the GoE's increasing share of block grant financing. However, there is no political economy analysis of the developmental state model and its implications for accountability mechanisms, civic space, or elite capture risks within the IGFT system. The document does not assess whether grievance redress mechanisms and social accountability interventions can function effectively under Ethiopia's constrained civic space, nor does it assess risks of political manipulation of woreda-level accountability structures. Government commitment assessment is financial and institutional rather than politically grounded.

dimensions.name: Objectives and Theory of Change
dimensions.rationale: The PDO addresses equity and decentralised accountability, which have indirect relevance to addressing grievances and social cohesion in a fragile context. However, the theory of change is entirely technocratic - it links IGFT flows to service delivery outcomes through accountability systems, with no explicit FCV logic. There is no articulation of how equitable service delivery reduces fragility drivers, builds social cohesion, or addresses trust deficits between citizens and the state. The choice of the P4R instrument is justified on institutional capacity-building grounds rather than FCV appropriateness.

dimensions.name: Design and Targeting
dimensions.rationale: Geographic equity targeting is a central design feature: the IGFT allocation formula explicitly directs higher per capita block grants to historically underdeveloped regions (including Gambella and Benishangul-Gumuz), and DLIs measure equity in NER and health outcomes across woredas. The bottom quintile targeting and explicit attention to geographic disparities are credible design responses to inequality. The ESSA is referenced as informing design and gender mainstreaming is incorporated. However, the targeting does not explicitly address FCV-affected populations (refugees, displaced persons, pastoral communities) and the design does not adapt components to higher-risk subnational areas.

dimensions.name: Implementation and Operational Flexibility
dimensions.rationale: Implementation arrangements are designed for a complex, decentralised, low-capacity environment and include capacity-building for environmental and social management at the woreda level, which is relevant to FCV risk management. The P4R instrument's use of government systems is justified by institutional strengthening rationale. However, there is no adaptation for security constraints in high-risk subnational areas, no third-party monitoring provision, no CERC or contingency mechanism, and no discussion of how the program would operate if access to certain regions was constrained by conflict or civil unrest. The post-Inspection Panel safeguards screening tool is operational but limited in scope.

dimensions.name: Results Framework and Adaptive Management
dimensions.rationale: The results framework includes geographic equity indicators (equity in NER, Penta3 outcomes) and an environmental and social screening indicator, which partially address FCV-relevant concerns. Independent verification agents are mandated for DLI verification, providing some M&E robustness. However, there are no FCV-specific indicators (e.g., measuring social cohesion, trust in government, or conflict-related exclusion), no disaggregation by displacement status or conflict-affected area, and no provision for adaptive management in response to deteriorating security conditions. The DLI framework is process-oriented rather than outcome-oriented on FCV dimensions.

dimensions.name: One WBG Integration (IFC/MIGA)
dimensions.rationale: IFC is mentioned only once in passing in the context of CPS Pillar 1 competitiveness objectives. MIGA is not mentioned. There is no discussion of private sector engagement, blended finance, or One WBG approaches in the context of this operation. Given that the operation focuses on public service delivery through government systems, IFC/MIGA engagement is not directly applicable at the operational level, but no attempt is made to connect the equity agenda to private sector job creation or economic inclusion in fragile subnational areas.

project_name: Urban Productive Safety Net Project (UPSNP)
sensitivity_rationale: The PAD provides solid poverty and labour market diagnostics but contains no explicit FCV conflict analysis, and the FCV cross-cutting tag is unchecked. Political governance risks are acknowledged but not linked to conflict drivers or fragility dynamics in the project's cities.
responsiveness_rationale: Design flexibility and phased learning are genuine strengths, but the theory of change, targeting design, results framework, and institutional linkages contain no FCV-specific adaptations. There is no contingency planning for conflict-related access disruptions and IFC/MIGA integration is absent.
key_finding: The UPSNP is a well-designed poverty-focused safety net with genuine adaptive mechanisms but no FCV framing — conflict context, do-no-harm analysis, and FCV-adjusted targeting and indicators are entirely absent. Three red flags (elite capture risk in CBT, potential IDP exclusion via residency threshold, and no FCV indicators) highlight meaningful gaps for a project operating across Ethiopia's most fragile cities.

dimensions.name: FCV Context and Diagnostics
dimensions.key_quote: Ethiopia is a large and geographically diverse country, with a representation of 98 nationalities who speak 93 languages... Ethiopia marked an important milestone in August 2012 with the first peaceful and constitutional transition of power.
dimensions.rationale: Country context is thorough on poverty and urbanisation but contains no FCV or conflict analysis. The FCV cross-cutting tag is explicitly unchecked in the PAD data sheet, and project cities in fragile regions (Gambella, Somali, Afar) receive no differentiated contextual treatment.

dimensions.name: Do No Harm and Conflict Risk
dimensions.key_quote: In the case of large covariate shocks affecting urban areas, the project will closely coordinate a required response with the Disaster Risk Management and Food Security Sector of the MoA, PSNP, and other programs.
dimensions.rationale: Social safeguards, a GRM, and governance/corruption risk mitigation (Annex 11) are in place. However, there is no conflict-sensitivity analysis, no assessment of how targeting or public works assignments could exacerbate inter-group or inter-community tensions, and no explicit do-no-harm framing.

dimensions.name: Stakeholder and Political Economy
dimensions.key_quote: The capacity building and program coordination and management component of the project will promote social accountability and active involvement of urban citizens in the planning, targeting, and implementing of the safety net.
dimensions.rationale: Citizens' engagement and social accountability are well-developed (Annex 10), and the 'developmental state' political economy is acknowledged. However, there is no mapping of politically sensitive stakeholder groups, no analysis of ethnic or political dynamics in fragile project cities, and no identification of potential spoilers.

dimensions.name: Objectives and Theory of Change
dimensions.key_quote: The UPSNP is grounded in the unique challenges that shape and perpetuate urban poverty... the project will put a significant premium on adaptation and learning, including establishing flexible arrangements that can be molded based on prevailing circumstances.
dimensions.rationale: The three-phase integrated pathway (transfers → livelihoods → graduation) is evidence-based and well-articulated. However, FCV-specific pathways — such as how safety nets contribute to social cohesion or resilience to conflict shocks — are entirely absent from the theory of change.

dimensions.name: Design and Targeting
dimensions.key_quote: Those who have been residing in their current place of residence for 6 months or more at the start of the targeting process will be considered for the program.
dimensions.rationale: The multi-layered targeting system (geographic, CBT, PMT, categorical, self-selection) is sophisticated and poverty-responsive. The 6-month residency threshold risks excluding IDPs and recent conflict-affected migrants in cities like Gambella and Jijiga, with no FCV-adjusted design provisions to address this.

dimensions.name: Implementation and Operational Flexibility
dimensions.key_quote: Learning and adaptation will be emphasized in years 1 and 2 and activities will gradually be scaled up to reach all eligible individuals... it is important to acknowledge that the proposed approach will be piloted, evaluated, course-corrected, and expanded as needed.
dimensions.rationale: Built-in piloting, phased scale-up, and food price early warning are genuine adaptive mechanisms. However, there are no contingency protocols for conflict-related access disruptions, no security escalation pathways, and no discussion of how operations would adapt if political conditions deteriorate in fragile project cities.

dimensions.name: Results Framework and Adaptive Management
dimensions.key_quote: Beneficiaries reporting that project investments reflected their needs (percentage), of which female (number) and male (number)... Project beneficiaries reporting increased household consumption as a result of the project.
dimensions.rationale: The results framework is gender-disaggregated and includes a rigorous impact evaluation design. No FCV-adjusted indicators are present — there are no social cohesion measures, conflict-sensitivity tracking indicators, or resilience metrics beyond consumption change.

dimensions.name: One WBG Integration
dimensions.key_quote: Joint IFC: No... To ensure synergy and complementarity with other ongoing projects in the urban environment, UPSNP will work closely with the ULGDP, the Competitiveness and Job Creation project and the WEDP.
dimensions.rationale: Annex 8 documents linkages to other WB urban programs (ULGDP, CJC, WEDP, PSNP), reflecting moderate WBG coordination. IFC and MIGA integration is explicitly absent, and there is no alignment with a broader WBG FCV strategy for Ethiopia.

project_name: Ethiopia: SME Finance Project
sensitivity_rationale: The PAD provides a detailed economic and financial sector context but contains no FCV diagnostics whatsoever. Ethiopia's ethnic diversity, nascent decentralisation tensions, and proximity to conflict-affected neighbours are noted only in neutral developmental terms. No conflict analysis, fragility framing, or do-no-harm assessment is present anywhere in the document.
responsiveness_rationale: The project PDO is narrowly transactional — increasing SME access to finance — with no FCV-informed theory of change, no conflict-sensitive targeting, and no adaptive management provisions for conflict or instability scenarios. Results indicators measure financial volumes and portfolio quality only; no FCV-adjusted metrics are present. IFC/MIGA integration is limited to a technical assistance role on leasing regulation.
key_finding: The Ethiopia SME Finance Project is a technically competent financial sector operation that contains no FCV analysis, conflict sensitivity, or fragility-adaptive design — notable given Ethiopia's multi-ethnic federal structure and the subnational tensions already visible at time of appraisal. The results framework and theory of change are entirely disconnected from the country's fragility dynamics.

dimensions.name: FCV Context and Diagnostics
dimensions.key_quote: Ethiopia is a large and geographically diverse country, with a representation of 98 nationalities and peoples who speak 93 languages... Ethiopia marked an important milestone in August 2012 with the first peaceful and constitutional transition of power.
dimensions.rationale: The country context is described entirely through an economic growth and financial sector development lens. Ethnic diversity and governance transition are mentioned but not analysed for FCV implications. No fragility assessment, no conflict driver analysis, and no reference to subnational tensions — including Oromia protests already emerging at the time of appraisal — are present.

dimensions.name: Do No Harm and Conflict Risk
dimensions.key_quote: Agricultural project as well as Mining and Quarries however are excluded from the project beneficiaries' sectors due to higher risk of potential negative impact on the environment.
dimensions.rationale: Sector exclusions are justified on environmental grounds only, with no conflict-sensitivity rationale. The SORT rates Political and Governance risk as only Moderate with no elaboration on conflict pathways. No analysis is made of whether channelling finance through state-owned DBE or state-linked MFIs could reinforce patronage networks or exacerbate inter-group inequalities.

dimensions.name: Stakeholder and Political Economy Analysis
dimensions.key_quote: The GoE has followed a 'developmental state' model with a strong role for the Government in certain aspects of the economy... State-owned banks constitute 70 percent of total assets of the banking sector.
dimensions.rationale: The PAD correctly identifies the dominant role of state-owned banks and the government's developmental state model, providing some implicit political economy framing. However, this analysis is not extended to assess elite capture risks, exclusion of ethnic or regional minority-owned enterprises, or the political economy of DBE's lending priorities in a diverse federal state.

dimensions.name: Objectives and Theory of Change
dimensions.key_quote: The project development objective is to increase access to finance for Eligible Small and Medium Enterprises in the Recipient's territory.
dimensions.rationale: The PDO and theory of change are purely financial sector development objectives. There is no acknowledgement of how improved SME finance could intersect with social stability, employment of conflict-affected or displaced populations, or regional equity. The theory of change (Figure 2.3 referenced in TOC) follows a linear financial intermediation logic with no FCV dimensions.

dimensions.name: Design and Targeting
dimensions.key_quote: The final project beneficiaries will be eligible SMEs operating in the manufacturing and agro-processing industries... SMEs in the tour and construction industries will not be eligible to receive working capital only.
dimensions.rationale: Targeting is based on sector and enterprise size criteria only. No geographic targeting of conflict-affected or lagging regions is proposed despite Ethiopia's significant subnational disparities. No provisions are made for SMEs owned by women in conflict-affected areas, displaced entrepreneurs, or enterprises in fragile peripheral regions such as Somali or Gambella regions.

dimensions.name: Implementation and Operational Flexibility
dimensions.key_quote: A Project Management Team (PMT) will be established in DBE with the direct responsibility of managing the credit facility... A Project Implementation Unit (PIU) will be established under the MoI.
dimensions.rationale: Implementation arrangements are standard for a financial intermediary project. No contingency provisions for political instability, conflict disruption, or civil unrest are included. The reliance on DBE branches and regional ReMSEDAs as entry points offers some geographic reach but no explicit conflict-sensitive operational protocols.

dimensions.name: Results Framework and Adaptive Management
dimensions.key_quote: The progress towards the PDO will be measured by the following indicators: (a) Volume of Financial Support to SMEs under the credit facility; (b) Number of SMEs reached with financial services; (c) Portfolio Quality under the credit facility.
dimensions.rationale: The results framework is entirely financial — volume, outreach, and portfolio quality. No FCV-sensitive indicators are present (e.g. share of SMEs in conflict-affected areas, employment of conflict-affected populations, geographic equity of lending). The impact evaluation design is rigorous but follows a standard treatment/control methodology with no conflict sensitivity lens.

dimensions.name: One WBG Integration (IFC/MIGA)
dimensions.key_quote: The WBG/IFC is currently providing technical assistance to the NBE in drafting all the necessary leasing directives and guidelines to supervise and regulate the leasing market in Ethiopia.
dimensions.rationale: IFC engagement is referenced in relation to leasing regulatory TA and is noted as complementary to the project's enabling environment component. This represents some degree of WBG coordination. However, this coordination is purely technical and financial sector focused; there is no evidence of FCV-oriented WBG integration or joint conflict-sensitivity analysis across IDA/IFC operations.

project_name: Eastern and Southern Africa Higher Education Centers of Excellence
sensitivity_rationale: The PAD contains no FCV diagnostic for any of the eight participating countries, including Ethiopia, which faces significant internal conflict dynamics. The 'Fragile, Conflict & Violence' cross-cutting topic checkbox is explicitly left unmarked. Political and governance risk is rated Substantial in the SORT but is framed entirely around governance capacity and multi-country coordination, not conflict or fragility. No do-no-harm or stakeholder political economy analysis is present.
responsiveness_rationale: The PDO, project design, and results framework are entirely devoid of FCV adaptation. The performance-based DLI/DLR financing model is robust for education accountability but contains no FCV-adjusted indicators or flexibility provisions for conflict-affected ACE locations. Burundi's exclusion due to unrest is noted factually but generates no design response. No IFC/MIGA integration, no adaptive management provisions tied to FCV risk.
key_finding: This regional higher education project treats all eight ESA participating countries as stable development contexts, with no FCV diagnostic, no conflict-sensitivity in design, and results indicators that measure only enrollment volumes and institutional partnerships. The competitive merit-based ACE selection model, while technically sound, risks concentrating benefits among already-advantaged institutions in capital cities while systematically excluding conflict-affected sub-national contexts and marginalised populations.

dimensions.name: FCV Context and Diagnostics
dimensions.key_quote: Fragile and/or Capacity Constraints [ ] ... Cross Cutting Topics: [ ] Fragile, Conflict & Violence
dimensions.rationale: The PAD provides no FCV context analysis for any of the eight participating countries. Ethiopia's ethnic and regional conflicts, Uganda's post-conflict north, and Tanzania's political tensions are completely absent from the strategic context. The only conflict reference is a passing mention that Burundi could not participate 'due to the unrest at that time,' with no further analysis.

dimensions.name: Do No Harm and Conflict Risk
dimensions.key_quote: Political and Governance: Substantial ... The risk is related to multi-country coordination challenges and varying commitment levels.
dimensions.rationale: The SORT rates political and governance risk as Substantial but frames this entirely around multi-country coordination, not conflict or harm pathways. No analysis of whether concentrating elite university resources in capital cities could exacerbate regional inequalities or exclusion in conflict-affected areas. No do-no-harm assessment is present.

dimensions.name: Stakeholder and Political Economy
dimensions.key_quote: Ten countries originally expressed interest in the project. Out of the ten countries, eight are participating in ACE II as ACE hosting countries.
dimensions.rationale: The PAD documents a competitive, merit-based selection process for ACEs involving broad stakeholder consultation. However, political economy analysis is limited to institutional buy-in and does not address power dynamics, elite capture risks in university governance, or how gender and ethnic exclusion patterns in higher education intersect with fragility. Burundi's exclusion due to conflict is noted without analysis.

dimensions.name: Objectives and Theory of Change
dimensions.key_quote: To strengthen selected Eastern and Southern African higher education institutions to deliver quality post-graduate education and build collaborative research capacity in the regional priority areas.
dimensions.rationale: The PDO and theory of change are grounded entirely in human capital and economic competitiveness logic. There is no acknowledgment that fragility, conflict, or social exclusion affect higher education access or outcomes in the region. The causal chain from ACE investment to regional development benefits assumes stable institutional environments that do not exist across all participating countries.

dimensions.name: Design and Targeting
dimensions.key_quote: Female enrollment rates (as a percent of total enrollment) are even lower with rates of less than one percent in Malawi and four percent in Ethiopia.
dimensions.rationale: The design includes gender disaggregation in enrollment targets and geographic balance across countries. However, targeting does not account for conflict-affected populations, internally displaced students, or universities in insecure locations. The competitive selection process may systematically exclude institutions in fragile sub-national contexts. No conflict-sensitivity in the beneficiary targeting framework.

dimensions.name: Implementation and Operational Flexibility
dimensions.key_quote: The Recipient shall cause the Africa Centers of Excellences to adopt not later than one month after the Effective Date... their respective PIPs in form and substance satisfactory to the Association.
dimensions.rationale: The DLI/DLR performance-based financing model creates disbursement rigidity that is not adapted to conflict or institutional disruption. Semi-annual NSC and RSC reviews provide some oversight flexibility but are not designed to respond to FCV events. No contingency provisions for sudden-onset conflict affecting ACE operations are visible in the extracted text.

dimensions.name: Results Framework and Adaptive Management
dimensions.key_quote: Regional students enrolled by the ACEs in Master's and PhD programs (number); Students enrolled by the ACEs in Master's and PhD programs (number); MoUs on partnerships (number); Accredited education programs (number).
dimensions.rationale: All PDO-level indicators are quantitative enrollment and partnership metrics with no FCV dimension. No indicators track access for conflict-affected or marginalised populations, institutional resilience to disruption, or conflict-sensitive research outputs. The results framework would register success even if ACE benefits accrued exclusively to elite, non-conflict-affected students in capital cities.

dimensions.name: One WBG Integration (IFC/MIGA)
dimensions.key_quote: Joint IFC: No
dimensions.rationale: No IFC or MIGA involvement in this operation. The PAD notes industry-academia partnership goals and private sector engagement as ACE activities, but there is no formal WBG integration with IFC instruments that could support private sector development in fragile contexts. No reference to MIGA political risk coverage for cross-border activities in FCV-affected countries.

project_name: PRODUCTIVE SAFETY NET PROJECT (PSNP 4) ADDITIONAL FINANCING
sensitivity_rationale: The PAD situates the PSNP within Ethiopia's broader vulnerability and shock landscape, including drought, food insecurity, and the El Nino response, and acknowledges significant within-region disparities and pastoral exclusion. However, the FCV context is framed almost entirely through a food security and poverty lens: conflict drivers, political economy risks, and inter-community tensions are absent from the country context section. Do-no-harm considerations are limited to ESMF screening of PW subprojects; no conflict sensitivity analysis is present. Stakeholder and political economy analysis is weak, with targeting risks in Afar and Somali acknowledged but without engagement with underlying governance or conflict dynamics.
responsiveness_rationale: The PDO is anchored in systemic effectiveness and scalability of the rural safety net, which is directly FCV-relevant given Ethiopia's chronic drought and shock exposure, and the design integrates PSNP and HFA into a common scalable framework. Component 3 invests in adaptive systems (early warning, e-payments, MIS, GRM) and pastoral targeting improvements, demonstrating operational flexibility. However, the theory of change does not explicitly link safety net design to conflict risk reduction or social cohesion, and the results framework lacks FCV-specific indicators. IFC/MIGA integration is absent and D8 scores low. Operational flexibility provisions (PW waivers in hotspot woredas, contingency budgets) are substantive but not labelled or framed as FCV adaptations.
key_finding: The PSNP Rural Safety Net PAD demonstrates strong operational responsiveness to drought and food insecurity shocks through scalable transfer mechanisms and adaptive design, but lacks an explicit FCV analytical lens: conflict drivers are absent from the country context, do-no-harm analysis is limited to ESMF safeguards, and the results framework contains no FCV-adjusted indicators. The exclusion of livelihood support from the most fragile pastoral regions (Afar and Somali) and absence of conflict sensitivity analysis in targeting design constitute the primary FCV gaps.

dimensions.name: FCV Context and Diagnostics
dimensions.key_quote: Falling—and converging—poverty rates across regions mask significant disparities within regions and among woredas... the El Nino-induced drought of 2016/17... almost 20 percent of the rural population required safety net support.
dimensions.rationale: The PAD provides a reasonably detailed socioeconomic and shock-vulnerability context, including drought frequency, food insecurity caseloads, and regional disparities. Ethiopia's SORT risk ratings include High Political and Governance risk, but the body of the document does not interrogate the political drivers of fragility or conflict dynamics in Afar and Somali. The diagnosis is primarily food security-driven; conflict, intercommunal violence, and state legitimacy are not discussed as structural risk factors shaping vulnerability.

dimensions.name: Do No Harm and Conflict Risk
dimensions.key_quote: PW subprojects will be screened using the GoE's Environmental and Social Management Framework (ESMF)... standard safeguard procedures will be applied to all PW subprojects.
dimensions.rationale: The project applies environmental and social safeguards (ESMF, Resettlement Policy Framework, Indigenous Peoples OP 4.10) and acknowledges risks of exclusion in pastoral areas. However, there is no explicit conflict sensitivity analysis or do-no-harm framework applied to transfer design, targeting, or PW activities. The potential for safety net transfers to create inter-community tensions, reinforce exclusion of conflict-affected groups, or be captured by local power structures in Afar and Somali is not assessed. The harm pathway of weaker implementation in fragile pastoral regions is named but not adequately mitigated.

dimensions.name: Stakeholder and Political Economy Analysis
dimensions.key_quote: Women's participation in various local-level program implementation structures... has led some women to move into other kebele leadership structures... A Gender Action Plan and Social Development Plan are included.
dimensions.rationale: The PAD includes a detailed gender analysis (Annex 5) and Social Development Plan (Annex 6), supported by a Gender Tag with indicators for gap analysis, targeted actions, and results monitoring. Community-based targeting processes and social accountability mechanisms (GRM, ESAP) reflect meaningful stakeholder engagement. However, the political economy of local power structures, elite capture risks in community targeting, and the role of regional governments in Afar and Somali as political actors rather than neutral implementers receive insufficient attention.

dimensions.name: Objectives and Theory of Change
dimensions.key_quote: The PDO is to support the Government of Ethiopia in improving the effectiveness and scalability of its rural safety net system... Improved scalability refers to consolidating the PSNP and HFA delivery systems into a single framework.
dimensions.rationale: The theory of change is coherent for food security and resilience: targeting chronically food insecure households, delivering timely and predictable transfers, and creating a scalable shock-response system. This implicitly addresses FCV-relevant risks such as drought-induced displacement and chronic vulnerability. However, FCV objectives are not explicit in the PDO or ToC; the project does not articulate a pathway through which the safety net contributes to social cohesion, state legitimacy, or conflict risk reduction. The ToC operates within a development framing without acknowledging how conflict dynamics might undermine or require adaptation of its core logic.

dimensions.name: Design and Targeting
dimensions.key_quote: Targeting accuracy of the PSNP has been considerably weaker in pastoral regions (specifically Afar and Somali)... The livelihood component will not roll out to Somali and Afar until evaluations show improvements.
dimensions.rationale: The design demonstrates context-sensitivity in several respects: variable transfer schedules for pastoral areas, PW waivers in nutrition hotspot woredas during severe droughts, differentiated support for labor-constrained households, and explicit acknowledgement of implementation gaps in Afar and Somali. Community-based targeting is supported by social accountability mechanisms. However, the deferral of livelihoods support from the most fragile pastoral regions risks deepening inequity, and the design does not specifically address conflict-affected or displacement-affected sub-populations within the program areas.

dimensions.name: Implementation and Operational Flexibility
dimensions.key_quote: In severe drought years, PW conditions will be waived for all households receiving safety net support... contingency budgets held at the woreda and federal levels... the program has the flexibility to provide extended months of support.
dimensions.rationale: The project has meaningful operational flexibility provisions: woreda-level contingency budgets, a scalability framework linking PSNP and HFA, PW waivers triggered by early warning hotspot classifications, and enhanced oversight for Afar and Somali including spot-checks and quarterly supervision missions. The Common Framework is an important adaptive mechanism. However, the implementation flexibility is framed around climate and drought shocks, not conflict or insecurity, and there are no provisions for conflict-related operational disruptions such as access constraints or security-related payment delays.

dimensions.name: Results Framework and Adaptive Management
dimensions.key_quote: Key results: % of core safety net transfers paid on time; # of PSNP core beneficiaries receiving e-payments; % of households in beneficiary list who are poor; % of PW subprojects meeting common standards.
dimensions.rationale: The results framework is technically well-designed for measuring safety net effectiveness (timeliness, targeting accuracy, payment modernisation, PW quality). Adaptive management provisions include an independent PSNP evaluation in 2018 to inform design modifications for pastoral regions. However, there are no FCV-specific indicators (e.g. social cohesion, grievance resolution rates, conflict-related service disruptions, or inclusion of conflict-affected populations). The GRM is mentioned but not tracked as an indicator. The absence of FCV-adjusted indicators in the results framework is a notable gap.

dimensions.name: One WBG Integration (IFC/MIGA)
dimensions.key_quote: Bank/IFC Collaboration: No.
dimensions.rationale: The datasheet explicitly records no IFC/MIGA collaboration. The PAD notes contributions from Finance and Markets and Social, Urban, Rural and Resilience Global Practices as contributing practice areas, but there is no discussion of private sector mobilisation, investment climate considerations, or IFC/MIGA engagement in fragile pastoral areas. Given the livelihood and microfinance dimensions of the project, there was an opportunity for IFC engagement (microfinance institutions are mentioned) but it was not pursued or documented.

project_name: Second Ethiopia Urban Water Supply and Sanitation Project
sensitivity_rationale: The PAD acknowledges a state of emergency and political unrest under SORT risks (High political and governance rating) but provides no structured FCV context analysis. No conflict or fragility diagnostic is present; the country context section focuses exclusively on economic growth, urbanization, and sector performance. Project sites include chronically fragile regions (Somali, Afar, Gambela, Tigray) but these receive no differentiated FCV-sensitive treatment.
responsiveness_rationale: The PDO and theory of change are standard urban service delivery constructs with no FCV adaptation. While the design includes a stepped eligibility approach and community consultation measures, these are efficiency-driven rather than conflict-sensitivity measures. Results indicators are generic WaSH metrics with no FCV-adjusted targets. The acknowledged political and governance risk (state of emergency) has no corresponding design mitigation beyond generic stakeholder coordination.
key_finding: The project explicitly acknowledges Ethiopia's state of emergency and High political/governance risk but treats this as a background condition rather than a design parameter, with no conflict-sensitivity analysis, no FCV-adjusted targeting for fragile secondary cities (Somali, Afar, Gambela regions), and no adaptive mechanisms for political instability. The results framework is entirely standard WaSH metrics with no FCV dimension.

dimensions.name: FCV Context and Diagnostics
dimensions.key_quote: Political and governance risks are rated high because of the recently declared state of emergency and political unrest.
dimensions.rationale: The SORT table rates political/governance risk as High, and paragraph 59 briefly mentions the state of emergency. However, there is no conflict analysis, fragility diagnostic, or reference to drivers of fragility. Secondary city sites include Jijiga and Degeh Bur (Somali Region), Semera (Afar), Gambela and Tigray towns — all with distinct FCV exposure — but no differentiated analysis is provided for these locations.

dimensions.name: Do No Harm and Conflict Risk
dimensions.key_quote: The involvement of various ministries and bureaus in the planning, implementation, management and regulation of urban water and sanitation... may pose substantial risk to project implementation.
dimensions.rationale: Do-no-harm considerations are entirely absent. There is no conflict-sensitive analysis of how water infrastructure investments in ethnically or politically contested cities might affect tensions. The resettlement policy framework (OP/BP 4.12 triggered) and social safeguards are addressed procedurally but not through a conflict lens. No harm pathway analysis for fragile secondary cities is present.

dimensions.name: Stakeholder and Political Economy
dimensions.key_quote: The process of tariff revisions also carries political risk, as political and social factors may delay or even prevent tariff increases.
dimensions.rationale: The PAD shows reasonable awareness of multi-stakeholder complexity — multiple ministries, utilities, regional bureaus, and communities — and designs coordination mechanisms through the National WaSH Steering Committee. Political economy risks around tariff setting are identified. However, no analysis of elite capture risks, ethnic or regional political dynamics, or how power asymmetries in target cities might affect benefit distribution is provided.

dimensions.name: Objectives and Theory of Change
dimensions.key_quote: The objective of the project is to increase access to enhanced water supply and sanitation services in an operationally efficient manner in Addis Ababa and selected secondary cities.
dimensions.rationale: The PDO is a standard service access and efficiency objective with no FCV dimension. The theory of change (infrastructure investment + institutional strengthening = improved service delivery) is plausible for a stable context but does not account for disruption from political instability or conflict. There is no pathway linking improved services to conflict prevention or social cohesion, which is a missed opportunity given the acknowledged state of emergency.

dimensions.name: Design and Targeting
dimensions.key_quote: Equity was taken into consideration in the selection of the participating cities... the project will facilitate the creation of job opportunities for women and youth through economic opportunities related to the management and operation of water and sanitation service delivery.
dimensions.rationale: The design includes pro-poor targeting for low-income areas (LIAs), gender-sensitive service delivery (women and youth managed latrines), and a stepped eligibility approach for secondary cities. These are positive design features. However, targeting logic is not informed by conflict sensitivity; cities with acute FCV exposure (Somali Region, Afar) are not given differentiated operational or financing arrangements to account for higher implementation risk.

dimensions.name: Implementation and Operational Flexibility
dimensions.key_quote: A resurgence of the unrest would impede implementation progress.
dimensions.rationale: The PAD acknowledges that political unrest could impede implementation but provides no contingency planning, adaptive mechanisms, or protocols for continuing service delivery during periods of instability. The DBO contracting model and performance-based agreements are designed for stable operating conditions. There are no provisions for pausing, redirecting, or modifying activities in response to conflict escalation in specific cities.

dimensions.name: Results Framework and Adaptive Management
dimensions.key_quote: The following indicators will be used to measure progress: People in urban areas whose excreta is safely managed... People in urban areas with access to improved water sources... Operation cost-coverage ratio... Savings from NRW interventions.
dimensions.rationale: All five PDO-level indicators are standard WaSH metrics. None are FCV-adjusted or include conflict-sensitive proxies (e.g., access equity across regions with different security profiles, service continuity during unrest). The midterm review is designed as a standard performance assessment with no FCV-lens evaluation. There is no adaptive management trigger linked to political or conflict risk escalation.

dimensions.name: One WBG Integration (IFC/MIGA)
dimensions.key_quote: Joint IFC: No
dimensions.rationale: No formal IFC or MIGA engagement is present in this project. However, the design actively supports private sector participation through DBO contracts, performance-based service management contracts, and a framework for small and micro enterprise involvement in sanitation service delivery. This de facto private sector development function could have benefited from formal IFC coordination, particularly for the commercial viability dimensions of utility reform.

project_name: Trade Logistics Project
sensitivity_rationale: The PAD provides no substantive analysis of FCV dynamics in Ethiopia. The country context section focuses exclusively on macroeconomic growth, trade competitiveness, and logistics infrastructure, with no mention of fragility drivers, conflict risks, or political economy of violence. Ethiopia's Somali Region tensions, intercommunal conflicts, and the political fragility of the ruling coalition — all highly relevant to a corridor project traversing contested territories — are entirely absent. The SORT table rates Political and Governance risk as 'Substantial' and Stakeholders as 'High' but does not connect either to FCV dynamics; do-no-harm considerations are limited to environmental and social safeguards for physical resettlement and are not extended to conflict sensitivity.
responsiveness_rationale: The project's theory of change, design, and results framework make no FCV-responsive adaptations. The PDO is expressed entirely in logistics efficiency terms (throughput, dwell time, container moves) with no indicators for inclusive access, conflict-sensitive implementation, or FCV-adjusted outcomes. The project concentrates $120M of $150M in a single physical facility with no mechanisms for adaptive management in response to political or conflict deterioration. Institutional design relies on a government-owned enterprise (ESLSE) with a monopoly on multimodal services — a dependency that creates vulnerability to political capture — yet no mitigation is articulated. One WBG integration is explicitly marked 'No' in the PAD data sheet.
key_finding: This trade logistics infrastructure project has no FCV sensitivity or responsiveness whatsoever: the country context is framed as a growth success story with conflict explicitly written off, and the design, theory of change, and all results indicators are standard logistics efficiency metrics with no FCV adaptation. The project's dependence on ESLSE — a state monopoly with well-documented governance constraints — raises elite capture concerns that are named but not mitigated.

dimensions.name: FCV Context and Diagnostics
dimensions.key_quote: Ethiopia has experienced rapid and stable economic growth over the past decade... the country avoided the volatility brought by spells of drought and conflict, which plagued growth in the past.
dimensions.rationale: The country context section explicitly frames Ethiopia as having overcome conflict and instability, without any recognition of ongoing fragility. No FCV diagnostic is provided: there is no reference to ethnic tensions, regional insurgencies (Ogaden/Somali region, Oromia unrest), or the political fragility of the EPRDF coalition. The corridor traverses politically sensitive territories but the PAD contains no conflict or fragility mapping whatsoever.

dimensions.name: Do No Harm and Conflict Risk
dimensions.key_quote: Social (including Safeguards)... Involuntary Resettlement OP/BP 4.12 [triggered]... The project will affect approximately 600 project affected persons.
dimensions.rationale: The PAD triggers social safeguards for involuntary resettlement and requires a Livelihood Restoration Plan, which shows some awareness of harm pathways. However, these are purely physical-displacement framed; there is no analysis of whether concentration of logistics infrastructure and ESLSE monopoly could generate resentment, exclusion of certain ethnic or regional business communities, or entrench state-business patronage networks. Conflict risk is not assessed; the SORT table notes 'Substantial' political and governance risk but does not connect it to do-no-harm design.

dimensions.name: Stakeholder and Political Economy Analysis
dimensions.key_quote: Logistics services in Ethiopia are dominated by ESLSE, a government-owned enterprise... Current government policy means that ESLSE is the only provider of multimodal services.
dimensions.rationale: The PAD identifies ESLSE's monopoly and regulatory barriers to competition as key sector constraints, which shows awareness of political economy dynamics. However, this analysis is framed through a market efficiency lens, not a fragility or elite capture lens. There is no analysis of who controls ESLSE, who benefits from the monopoly, whether the project could reinforce existing power asymmetries, or how ethnic and regional political economy dynamics shape logistics sector governance. Stakeholder risk is rated 'High' in SORT but is not unpacked in FCV terms.

dimensions.name: Objectives and Theory of Change
dimensions.key_quote: The objective of this project is to enhance the performance of the Ethio-Djibouti corridor through improvements in operational capacity, efficiency, and range of logistics services at the Modjo Dry Port.
dimensions.rationale: The PDO is narrowly focused on logistics efficiency at a single node. The theory of change does not incorporate FCV dynamics as either a risk to the change pathway or as an entry point for programming. There is no acknowledgment that political instability, ethnic conflict, or governance fragility could disrupt the corridor or undermine project outcomes, nor is resilience-building or conflict-sensitive infrastructure framing incorporated into the objectives.

dimensions.name: Design and Targeting
dimensions.key_quote: Project beneficiaries will primarily be private sector exporters, importers, manufacturers and farmers... New and better jobs created for local residents.
dimensions.rationale: The project identifies broad beneficiary categories including smallholder farmers and SMEs, which has some pro-poor orientation. However, no targeting logic accounts for FCV-affected populations, marginalised regions, or conflict-displaced communities. The $120M infrastructure concentration at Modjo serves primarily the Addis Ababa industrial and commercial class; there is no disaggregation of benefits by region, ethnicity, or conflict exposure. The design is standard infrastructure project design with no FCV-responsive adaptations.

dimensions.name: Implementation and Operational Flexibility
dimensions.key_quote: The project comprises a mix of public infrastructure at the Modjo Dry Port, coordinated with targeted investment in ICT and regulatory and administrative reforms.
dimensions.rationale: The project has no documented mechanisms for adaptive management in response to political or security deterioration. Implementation relies heavily on EMAA and ESLSE — government entities whose operational capacity is described as a 'Substantial' risk — without contingency arrangements. There are no provisions for geographic or operational pivots if security conditions along the corridor deteriorate, and no crisis-response protocols are described.

dimensions.name: Results Framework and Adaptive Management
dimensions.key_quote: PDO level indicators: (a) Throughput of Modjo Dry Port (total tons per year); (b) Container dwell time in Modjo Dry Port (days); (c) Moves within Modjo facility (number per hour); (d) Containers returning to Djibouti stuffed with exports (number per year).
dimensions.rationale: All four PDO-level indicators are purely operational logistics metrics. None are FCV-adjusted, conflict-sensitive, or inclusive in design. There are no indicators tracking access by marginalised groups, conflict-related disruptions to operations, or governance improvements with FCV relevance. The M&E framework does not include a mechanism for tracking conflict dynamics or adjusting implementation in response to security deterioration.

dimensions.name: One WBG Integration (IFC/MIGA)
dimensions.key_quote: Joint IFC: No
dimensions.rationale: The PAD data sheet explicitly records no IFC joint engagement. Given the project's focus on private sector logistics, regulatory reform, and ESLSE monopoly reduction — all areas where IFC private sector instruments could have strengthened the FCV-responsiveness of the design — this absence is a missed opportunity. No MIGA instruments are referenced. There is no discussion of IFC or MIGA support to private logistics operators, which could have provided an alternative to state-dominated service delivery.

project_name: Livestock and Fisheries Sector Development Project
sensitivity_rationale: The PAD briefly notes 2016 political unrest, a lifted state of emergency, and ethnic tensions on the Somali-Oromia border, but treats these as background rather than as inputs to a systematic fragility or conflict analysis. No FCV diagnostic framework is applied, and the links between Ethiopia's conflict drivers (resource competition, ethnic federalism, pastoral land tenure) and livestock sector dynamics are not explored. The SORT rates Political and Governance risk as High, but this finding is not translated into conflict-sensitive design choices.
responsiveness_rationale: The project's theory of change is grounded in agricultural development logic and does not integrate FCV dynamics. The CERC sub-component provides emergency response flexibility, and IFC linkage is documented. However, the pastoral lowlands — Ethiopia's primary conflict-prone areas — are explicitly excluded from Component A without a conflict-sensitivity rationale. The results framework has no FCV-specific indicators, and implementation arrangements do not include conflict monitoring or adaptive management mechanisms linked to the security environment.
key_finding: This livestock and fisheries development project operates across conflict-affected regions of Ethiopia with a High SORT political risk rating, yet applies no systematic FCV analysis to its design — pastoral exclusion from Component A is not conflict-sensitivity assessed, the results framework contains no FCV-adjusted indicators, and the theory of change does not account for how fragility dynamics could undermine the agricultural transformation pathway.

dimensions.name: FCV Context and Diagnostics
dimensions.key_quote: Political unrest in 2016 has largely settled but pockets of uncertainty remain. Strikes and signs of unrest erupted in some parts linked to ethnic tensions on the borders between Somali and Oromia regions.
dimensions.rationale: The PAD acknowledges political unrest and ethnic tensions in passing, but offers no structured FCV or conflict analysis. There is no fragility classification, no conflict driver mapping, and no assessment of how Ethiopia's structural drivers of fragility (ethnic federalism, land tenure, pastoralist-highland tensions, drought-conflict nexus) relate to the project's operational context. The SCD is referenced for economic growth logic, not fragility analysis.

dimensions.name: Do No Harm and Conflict Risk
dimensions.key_quote: Special attention will be given to include underserved peoples and vulnerable groups as potential beneficiaries as identified in the Social Assessment (Social Development Plan, see PAD Annex 7).
dimensions.rationale: The project triggers multiple safeguard policies including OP 4.10 (Indigenous Peoples) and OP 4.12 (Involuntary Resettlement), and prepares a Social Development Plan — indicating awareness of exclusion risks. However, no explicit analysis is conducted of how livestock value chain investments in communal grazing areas, or the exclusion of pastoral lowlands from Component A, could exacerbate inter-ethnic resource competition. The CERC component signals crisis awareness but does not substitute for a conflict risk assessment.

dimensions.name: Stakeholder and Political Economy
dimensions.key_quote: The project will support inclusive targeting of those groups for them to access productive assets. Consensus building to ensure that new policies reflect the views and needs of women, youth, smallholder farmers and larger participants.
dimensions.rationale: The PAD acknowledges the political economy of the newly established MoLF and the need for institutional capacity building. Policy dialogue and stakeholder consultation processes are planned for Sub-component B.2. However, a political economy analysis of power dynamics within the livestock value chain — including how cooperative structures could be captured by local elites or how ethnic identity intersects with market access — is absent. The treatment of pastoralist communities as a distinct stakeholder group with specific conflict-related concerns is not undertaken.

dimensions.name: Objectives and Theory of Change
dimensions.key_quote: Increase productivity and commercialization of producers and processors in selected value chains, strengthen service delivery systems in the livestock and fisheries sectors, and respond promptly and effectively to an eligible crisis or emergency.
dimensions.rationale: The PDO includes an explicit crisis response element through the CERC sub-component, which represents a meaningful concession to Ethiopia's emergency-prone context. The transformation pathway is coherent for development purposes. However, the theory of change does not account for how FCV dynamics (conflict, political instability, displacement) might affect the causal pathway from inputs to outcomes, nor does it articulate how the project would adapt if the security environment deteriorated.

dimensions.name: Design and Targeting
dimensions.key_quote: The arid and semi-arid lowland regions, where pastoralism dominates, are already supported by ongoing IDA-funded projects and therefore are not being considered here under component A.
dimensions.rationale: The project targets smallholders, women-headed households, and unemployed youth, which reflects a poverty focus relevant to vulnerability. The geographic footprint includes Gambella and Benishangul-Gumuz — both conflict-affected regions — but their inclusion appears to follow agro-ecological suitability criteria rather than an FCV-informed targeting rationale. The deliberate exclusion of the pastoral lowlands from Component A, while administratively logical, is not analysed for its potential do-no-harm implications given the pastoralist-highland tensions in Ethiopia.

dimensions.name: Implementation and Operational Flexibility
dimensions.key_quote: Sub-component B.5. Contingent Emergency Response aims to support immediate and rapid response to an Eligible Crisis or Emergency, as needed.
dimensions.rationale: The CERC sub-component (B.5) provides a formal mechanism for rapid reallocation of funds in response to a crisis, which is a genuine flexibility provision appropriate for Ethiopia's context. Multi-level PCUs down to Woreda level provide decentralised implementation capacity. However, there are no contingency plans specific to security deterioration, no provisions for staff safety or remote monitoring in conflict-affected areas, and no guidance on how implementation sequencing would adapt if violence escalated in project areas.

dimensions.name: Results Framework and Adaptive Management
dimensions.key_quote: Time lapse between early warning information and disbursement toward response, in case of crisis or emergency (in weeks).
dimensions.rationale: The results framework includes five PDO-level indicators: productivity change, sales increase, service delivery improvements, training application, and CERC response time. Gender indicators are included. However, there are no FCV-adjusted indicators — no measures of conflict sensitivity, social cohesion, community trust in institutions, or context change relevant to the fragility dynamics identified in the country context. The CERC response-time indicator addresses emergency speed but not the broader adaptive management of FCV risks.

dimensions.name: One WBG Integration (IFC/MIGA)
dimensions.key_quote: Bank/IFC Collaboration: Yes — Complementary or Interdependent project requiring active coordination. IFC non-lending advisory services (Livestock Micro-Reform for Agribusiness — L-MIRA) support regulatory reform and capacity building.
dimensions.rationale: The PAD documents an active IFC linkage through the L-MIRA project, which provides regulatory reform and capacity building to Ethiopia's veterinary drugs and feed control authority. This is described as a complementary and interdependent relationship requiring active coordination. The WBG integration is genuine and relevant to private sector development in the livestock sector, though it does not extend to FCV-specific private sector engagement or conflict-sensitive investment screening.

project_name: Ethiopia: National Quality Infrastructure Development Project
sensitivity_rationale: The PAD acknowledges Ethiopia's state of emergency (October 2016) in a single sentence but makes no analytical use of this information. There is no FCV context analysis, no conflict diagnostics, and no engagement with the drivers of the anti-government protests that prompted the emergency. Political and governance risk is rated Substantial in the SORT, but the narrative frames this as an institutional governance issue, not a conflict-fragility dynamic. The project entirely omits analysis of how ethnic diversity (98 nationalities), regional inequalities, or political tensions could affect or be affected by NQI development.
responsiveness_rationale: The project design is a standard competitiveness and institutional capacity-building operation with no FCV-adaptive features. Targeting is sector-based (leather, textiles, agro-processing) with no consideration of how geographic or ethnic exclusion risks might manifest. There are no conflict-sensitive implementation provisions, no adaptive management triggers, and no FCV-adjusted results indicators. IFC/MIGA collaboration is explicitly marked absent. The theory of change assumes a stable institutional environment and makes no provision for the conflict-fragility context acknowledged at the outset of the document.
key_finding: This is a standard competitiveness project with negligible FCV integration: despite operating under an active state of emergency, the PAD contains no FCV diagnostics, no do-no-harm analysis, and no conflict-sensitive design features. The project's sector targeting, institutional assumptions, and results framework are entirely disconnected from Ethiopia's fragility context.

dimensions.name: FCV Context and Diagnostics
dimensions.key_quote: the country is in a nationwide state of emergency declared by the Government of Ethiopia (GoE) on October 9, 2016 for six months in light of growing concerns related to internal security following months of anti-government protests.
dimensions.rationale: The PAD identifies the state of emergency in the Country Context section but treats it as background scenery rather than a substantive analytical input. No conflict drivers, fragility indicators, or FCV diagnostic framework is applied. The document contains no reference to ACLED, World Bank FCV classifications, or any systematic analysis of how political instability, ethnic tensions, or regional grievances interact with the NQI development agenda.

dimensions.name: Do No Harm and Conflict Risk
dimensions.key_quote: Political and Governance: Substantial [SORT rating]. Macroeconomic: Moderate. Overall: Substantial.
dimensions.rationale: The SORT assigns Substantial risk to political governance but the risk narrative focuses on bureaucratic implementation risks, not conflict or FCV-specific harm pathways. There is no do-no-harm analysis examining whether NQI investments could exacerbate regional inequalities, benefit politically connected enterprises, or inadvertently reinforce exclusionary economic structures. No conflict risk mitigation is proposed beyond standard fiduciary safeguards.

dimensions.name: Stakeholder and Political Economy
dimensions.key_quote: The direct beneficiaries of the project interventions include the private sector (particularly existing and potential exporters), private NQI service providers, regulatory authorities, and consumers.
dimensions.rationale: The project includes a structured private sector engagement component (Component 2) and a feedback/dialogue mechanism (Subcomponent 2.3), which shows some attention to stakeholder dynamics. However, there is no political economy analysis of who controls access to NQI services, no examination of elite capture risks in a context of concentrated industrial ownership, and no consideration of how Ethiopia's ethnic federalism and regional economic disparities might shape who benefits from quality infrastructure development.

dimensions.name: Objectives and Theory of Change
dimensions.key_quote: The theory of change for the proposed project is that both improved delivery of quality assurance services by NQI institutions (supply side) and increased demand by targeted enterprises in the targeted sectors... will strengthen the competitiveness of the selected industries in GVCs.
dimensions.rationale: The theory of change is coherent for a standard competitiveness project but is entirely blind to FCV context. It assumes stable government institutions, predictable private sector demand, and a permissive operating environment — none of which are assured given the concurrent state of emergency. There is no contingency logic for how project objectives would be pursued or revised in conditions of political instability or conflict escalation.

dimensions.name: Design and Targeting
dimensions.key_quote: These three value chains have been selected as priority sectors under the project for the following main reasons: (a) the GoE has clearly identified them in GTP II as its priority sectors for the development of export-led manufacturing industries.
dimensions.rationale: Sector targeting follows government industrial policy priorities without any conflict-sensitive lens. No analysis is provided of whether geographic concentration of beneficiaries in Addis Ababa or industrial parks could exacerbate regional tensions. The Gender Action Plan notes some gender-disaggregated indicators but does not connect gender exclusion to FCV dynamics. Vulnerable or conflict-affected populations are entirely absent from the targeting rationale.

dimensions.name: Implementation and Operational Flexibility
dimensions.key_quote: MoST will need to strengthen its capacity to effectively implement this project. The Ministry does not have prior experience implementing IDA financed project.
dimensions.rationale: Implementation arrangements are standard PIU-based with no FCV-adaptive features. There are no provisions for alternative implementation pathways if political instability disrupts MoST operations. The acknowledged capacity gap at MoST, combined with an active state of emergency, creates significant implementation risk that is not addressed through flexibility mechanisms, simplified procedures, or conflict-contingency plans.

dimensions.name: Results Framework and Adaptive Management
dimensions.key_quote: Number of enterprises that used NQI institutions' quality assurance services (disaggregated by sector). Average number of days required to obtain selected calibration and testing services.
dimensions.rationale: The results framework is purely technical and sector-oriented with no FCV-sensitive indicators. There are no indicators tracking conflict risk, social inclusion, geographic equity of service access, or institutional resilience. The M&E system is designed to track output delivery, not the project's interaction with the fragility context. No adaptive management triggers are defined for scenario-based responses to political or security deterioration.

dimensions.name: One WBG Integration (IFC/MIGA)
dimensions.key_quote: Bank/IFC Collaboration: No
dimensions.rationale: IFC and MIGA collaboration is explicitly absent. Given the project's stated goal of building private sector NQI service providers and improving export competitiveness — areas where IFC private sector advisory and investment could add significant value — the absence of IFC engagement represents a missed opportunity. No coordination with IFC's SME finance or trade facilitation work in Ethiopia is noted despite several complementary operations being referenced.

project_name: General Education Quality Improvement Program Phase 2 - GPE 2 Grant
sensitivity_rationale: The document provides basic country context (poverty, decentralization post-Derg, pastoralist regions) but does not analyse Ethiopia's conflict or fragility dynamics. No conflict diagnostics, no reference to ethnic tensions, regional insurgencies, or FCV drivers. The treatment of Somali and Afar regions as underserved areas is framed solely as an equity-access issue, not a fragility or conflict concern.
responsiveness_rationale: The program's theory of change and design are conventional education sector interventions with no FCV-adaptive logic. Targeting of vulnerable groups (pastoralists, girls, children with disabilities) provides partial responsiveness, but implementation arrangements, results frameworks, and the overall PDO contain no conflict-sensitive provisions. IFC/MIGA integration is absent. RF5 deduction applied for results framework lacking any FCV-adjusted indicators.
key_finding: GEQIP Phase 1 (as extracted) is a well-designed education quality program that explicitly targets marginalised populations but operates entirely outside an FCV analytical frame — conflict, fragility, and violence are not diagnosed, and the results framework contains no FCV-adjusted indicators. The program's equity-focused design provides a partial foundation for FCV responsiveness that is not built upon.

dimensions.name: FCV Context and Diagnostics
dimensions.key_quote: Access to education opportunities continues to be an obstacle, especially for females and other 'most vulnerable children', poor students and pastoral areas (e.g., Somali and Afar).
dimensions.rationale: The document provides a poverty and development context but does not engage with Ethiopia's conflict or fragility landscape. Disadvantaged regions (Somali, Afar) are identified as access gaps, not as conflict-affected or fragile areas. No analysis of how FCV dynamics drive education deprivation.

dimensions.name: Do No Harm and Conflict Risk
dimensions.key_quote: Does the project include any critical risks rated 'substantial' or 'high'? [X] Yes.
dimensions.rationale: The risk table references substantial/high risks but these relate to institutional capacity, fiduciary performance, and government ownership — not conflict or fragility risks. There is no do-no-harm analysis, no conflict-risk screening, and no mitigation of potential harm from uneven regional resource flows in a multi-ethnic context.

dimensions.name: Stakeholder and Political Economy
dimensions.key_quote: The possibility that GEQIP funding (as part of PBS) may be cut or suspended in the event of any 'political' issues between the donor community and the Government.
dimensions.rationale: Political economy is partially acknowledged — donor-government political dynamics, government ownership, and institutional performance incentives are discussed. However, analysis does not extend to intra-Ethiopian political economy, ethnic federalism tensions, or elite capture risks within specific conflict-affected regions.

dimensions.name: Objectives and Theory of Change
dimensions.key_quote: The development objectives of Phase 1 of the GEQIP are to: (i) improve teaching and learning conditions in primary and secondary education; and (ii) improve management planning and budget capacity of the MOE and Regional Bureaus.
dimensions.rationale: The PDO and theory of change are standard education quality improvement objectives with no FCV adjustment. There is no acknowledgement that conflict or fragility conditions require differentiated approaches in emerging or conflict-affected regions. The program logic does not account for how FCV dynamics may undermine educational outcomes.

dimensions.name: Design and Targeting
dimensions.key_quote: The most vulnerable children in Ethiopia are 'rural & pastoralist children, orphans, children in remote locations, street children and drop outs', as well as those with disabilities.
dimensions.rationale: The project explicitly targets the most marginalised populations including pastoralists (Somali, Afar), girls, and children with disabilities. Alternative Basic Education (ABE) for out-of-school children and gender budgeting are designed in. However, targeting rationale is equity-driven rather than FCV-adaptive — there is no differentiation for conflict-affected zones or adjustment for insecurity-driven exclusion.

dimensions.name: Implementation and Operational Flexibility
dimensions.key_quote: A flexible approach in design so that resources are provided to MOE and implementing units at the national level and regions based on their work plans; base costs under the components are indicative only.
dimensions.rationale: The APL instrument and annual work plan approach provide general operational flexibility. Budget allocations are indicative and adjustable. However, there is no conflict-contingency mechanism, no provision for accelerated implementation in stable areas while pausing in insecure ones, and no regional differentiation for fragile or conflict-affected contexts.

dimensions.name: Results Framework and Adaptive Management
dimensions.key_quote: A gender and equity needs assessment that covers all of the GEQIP components during Year 1 will be developed and implemented.
dimensions.rationale: The results framework includes learning assessments, EMIS data, school grants utilization surveys, and gender/equity needs assessments. These provide a solid monitoring base for an education project. However, no FCV-adjusted indicators are included — there are no indicators tracking conflict-related disruptions, displacement effects on enrollment, or security-conditional implementation progress.

dimensions.name: One WBG Integration (IFC/MIGA)
dimensions.key_quote: The proposed support from IDA will be provided within the framework and principles of a thematic Sector Wide-Approach (SWAp) through a pooled funding mechanism.
dimensions.rationale: No IFC or MIGA involvement is mentioned or referenced. The program is a pooled IDA/bilateral DP operation. There is no private sector engagement strategy and no attempt to leverage IFC instruments for education service delivery in fragile regions.

project_name: Enhancing Shared Prosperity through Equitable Services
